title: Norman Conquest
summary: William of Normandy's victory reordered rulership, landholding, and aristocratic power.
publicSummary: William of Normandy's victory changed who ruled, who owned land, and who held power.

title: Domesday Book
summary: A remarkably detailed survey of land and resources that strengthened fiscal and administrative knowledge.
publicSummary: A detailed survey of land and resources gave the crown much stronger knowledge for taxing and ruling.

title: Becket Crisis
summary: Conflict between Henry II and Thomas Becket dramatized tension between royal and ecclesiastical authority.
publicSummary: Henry II's conflict with Thomas Becket showed how sharply royal power could clash with church authority.

title: Magna Carta
summary: A baronial settlement that became a foundational symbol of limiting arbitrary rule.
publicSummary: A baronial deal that became a lasting symbol of limiting arbitrary royal power.

title: Simon de Montfort's Parliament
summary: An important step in the development of representative parliamentary politics.
publicSummary: An early step toward more representative parliamentary politics.

title: Model Parliament
summary: A key moment in consolidating the representative shape of Parliament.
publicSummary: A key moment in shaping Parliament into a more representative body.

title: Black Death
summary: Plague mortality transformed demography, labour relations, and social confidence.
publicSummary: Plague killed on a huge scale and changed population, labour, and social confidence.

title: Peasants' Revolt
summary: A major uprising revealing the explosive politics of tax, labour, and hierarchy after plague.
publicSummary: A major revolt that showed how explosive tax, labour, and hierarchy had become after plague.

title: Losses in France and political crisis
summary: Military failure abroad intensified domestic weakness and elite blame.
publicSummary: Military failure overseas deepened weakness at home and sharpened elite blame.

title: Wars of the Roses
summary: Dynastic conflict exposed the fragility of legitimacy and noble consensus.
publicSummary: Dynastic conflict exposed how fragile public trust and noble agreement really were.

title: Act of Supremacy
summary: The English monarch was declared supreme head of the Church of England.
publicSummary: The monarch was declared head of the Church of England.

title: Dissolution of the Monasteries
summary: Monastic houses were suppressed, redistributing wealth and reshaping local welfare and piety.
publicSummary: Monasteries were closed, shifting wealth and changing local care, worship, and daily life.

title: Elizabethan Religious Settlement
summary: A Protestant settlement sought political stability while retaining coercive control.
publicSummary: A Protestant religious order tried to steady politics while still using coercion.

title: Union of the Crowns
summary: James VI and I brought the English and Scottish crowns together under one monarch.
publicSummary: James VI and I united the English and Scottish crowns under one ruler.

title: English Civil Wars
summary: War over sovereignty, religion, and authority shattered the old political settlement.
publicSummary: War over who should rule, what to believe, and where authority lay shattered the old order.

title: Bill of Rights
summary: The post-1688 settlement firmly limited monarchy and strengthened parliamentary principles.
publicSummary: After 1688, monarchy was more firmly limited and parliamentary rule grew stronger.

title: Acts of Union
summary: England and Scotland were united in the Kingdom of Great Britain.
publicSummary: England and Scotland were formally joined in the Kingdom of Great Britain.

title: Seven Years' War and imperial expansion
summary: Victory strengthened Britain's global reach while deepening debt and imperial entanglement.
publicSummary: Victory expanded Britain's global power, while also deepening debt and imperial involvement.

title: First Reform Act
summary: The antiquated electoral system was partly reworked, beginning a longer age of reform.
publicSummary: The old electoral system was partly reworked, opening a longer era of reform.

title: Factory Act
summary: The state began more direct regulation of industrial labour conditions, especially for children.
publicSummary: The state began regulating factory conditions more directly, especially for children.
destroyedOrDisplaced: pure laissez-faire assumptions in some sectors

title: Third Reform Act
summary: Voting rights widened substantially, deepening mass politics.
publicSummary: Voting rights widened sharply, deepening mass politics.

title: First and Second World Wars
summary: Total war reshaped the economy, state power, citizenship, gender roles, and expectations of social protection.
publicSummary: Two world wars reshaped the economy, state power, citizenship, gender roles, and expectations of protection.

title: Creation of the NHS and welfare settlement
summary: The postwar settlement institutionalised broad social protection and universal health care.
publicSummary: The postwar welfare state made broad social protection and universal health care part of everyday expectation.

title: Thatcher era reforms
summary: Privatisation, deregulation, anti-union reforms, and market liberalisation reshaped the economy and state.
publicSummary: Privatisation, deregulation, anti-union change, and market reform reshaped the economy and the state.

title: Good Friday Agreement
summary: A power-sharing settlement largely ended the Troubles and created devolved institutions in Northern Ireland.
publicSummary: A power-sharing deal largely ended the Troubles and created devolved government in Northern Ireland.
destroyedOrDisplaced: some assumptions of zero-sum sovereignty politics

title: Brexit and exit from the EU
summary: The referendum and withdrawal from the EU exposed sovereignty conflict, regional difference, and long-running legitimacy strain.
publicSummary: The Brexit vote and EU withdrawal exposed deep arguments over sovereignty, region, and trust in the system.

title: Expulsion of the Jews from England
summary: Edward I expelled England's Jewish communities, binding fiscal extraction, confessional exclusion, and a harsher social hierarchy into the political order.
publicSummary: Edward I expelled England's Jewish communities, tightening exclusion, extraction, and a harsher social hierarchy.
destroyedOrDisplaced: Jewish communal life in medieval England

title: Spanish Armada
summary: The failed Spanish Armada sharpened Protestant state identity, intensified military preparation, and reinforced England's sense of being locked in a confessional struggle.
publicSummary: The failed Armada sharpened Protestant identity, intensified military preparation, and deepened the sense of religious struggle.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the prospect of easy confessional settlement with Catholic Europe

title: Plantation of Ulster
summary: State-backed plantation reordered land, settlement, and power in Ulster, turning confessional difference and hierarchy into a more durable political fault line.
publicSummary: State-backed plantation changed land, power, and population in Ulster, turning religious difference into a lasting political fault line.
destroyedOrDisplaced: older landholding balances and local trust

title: Harrying of the North
summary: The crown's brutal suppression in the north fused conquest, punitive destruction, and a harsher social order into the early Norman state.
publicSummary: The crown's brutal suppression in the north fused conquest, destruction, and a harsher social order into the new Norman state.
destroyedOrDisplaced: regional autonomy and material security in the north

title: Anselm and the Investiture Conflict
summary: Conflict over appointment, obedience, and ecclesiastical authority exposed how tightly kingship and sacred order were bound together in the new regime.
publicSummary: Conflict over appointments and church authority showed how tightly kingship and sacred order were tied together.
consequences: continued royal negotiation with sacred legitimacy
madePossible: a more negotiated settlement between crown and church
destroyedOrDisplaced: easy fusion of royal will and clerical obedience

title: Crusading Taxation and Richard's Ransom
summary: Heavy taxation for crusade and ransom intensified extraction from below and sharpened resentment at a more expensive martial kingship.
publicSummary: Heavy taxation for crusade and ransom deepened extraction and sharpened resentment at costly royal warfare.
consequences: greater linkage between war and taxation
destroyedOrDisplaced: some tolerance for exceptional royal demands

title: Papal Interdict and John's Submission
summary: The papal interdict and John's eventual submission exposed the vulnerability of royal legitimacy when sacred and political authority collided.
publicSummary: The papal interdict and John's submission showed how vulnerable royal trust could become when church and crown collided.
destroyedOrDisplaced: the image of unconstrained royal authority

title: Oldcastle Rebellion and Anti-Heresy Repression
summary: The failed rising associated with Lollardy reinforced the link between political order and religious conformity in a period already anxious about loyalty.
publicSummary: The failed rising tied political order even more tightly to religious conformity in an anxious age.
consequences: stronger fusion of order and orthodoxy
destroyedOrDisplaced: some tolerance for heterodox reform currents

title: Jack Cade's Rebellion
summary: Popular revolt against corruption, misrule, and material strain revealed how elite failure and social grievance were beginning to converge.
publicSummary: Popular revolt against corruption and hardship showed elite failure and social anger starting to come together.
madePossible: sharper articulation of grievance against misrule

title: Crécy campaign and capture of Calais
summary: Edward III's campaign brought victory at Crécy and the capture of Calais, tying military success to heavy fiscal strain and a longer war footing.
publicSummary: Victory at Crécy and the capture of Calais tied military success to heavy taxation and a longer war footing.
consequences: a durable English foothold at Calais
madePossible: a continental base for future campaigns
destroyedOrDisplaced: any easy hope of a cheap or short war
sourcesOrRationale: Based on Britannica entries on Edward III, the Hundred Years' War, and the Battle of Crécy.

title: Statute of Labourers
summary: The crown tried to hold wages down and restrict labour movement after the Black Death, turning post-plague scarcity into a sharper struggle over work, status, and coercion.
publicSummary: After the Black Death, the crown tried to hold wages down and restrict labour movement, sharpening the struggle over work and coercion.
consequences: deeper resentment among workers and tenants
madePossible: stronger legal enforcement of labour hierarchy
destroyedOrDisplaced: some of the bargaining power created by labour scarcity
sourcesOrRationale: Based on the Statute of Labourers text via Britannica/Avalon and The National Archives overview.

title: Statutes of Provisors and Praemunire
summary: Legislation against papal appointments and appeals to Rome reflected hostility to outside clerical authority and reinforced a harder link between political order and religious control.
publicSummary: Laws against papal influence hardened the link between political order and tighter religious control.
consequences: greater royal control over patronage disputes
narrower room for external ecclesiastical influence
madePossible: more national control over church appointments and legal appeals
destroyedOrDisplaced: some scope for papal intervention in English church affairs
sourcesOrRationale: Based on Britannica entries on Edward III and the Statute of Provisors.

title: Conquest of Wales and Statute of Wales
summary: Edward I's Welsh campaigns and the subsequent Statute of Wales bound military conquest to administrative integration, extending English crown power through castles, shires, and direct rule.
publicSummary: Edward I's conquest of Wales tied military victory to tighter administration, extending crown power through castles and direct rule.
consequences: destruction of an independent Welsh principality
stronger English administrative control in Wales
longer-term tension around conquest and incorporation
madePossible: deeper territorial integration under the English crown
destroyedOrDisplaced: the remaining autonomy of native Welsh princely rule
sourcesOrRationale: Based on Britannica's coverage of Edward I's Welsh campaigns, the Treaty of Conway, and the Statute of Wales.

title: Gunpowder Plot
summary: The failed attempt to destroy king and Parliament turned confessional fear into harder state suspicion, sharpening anti-Catholic identity and loyalty politics under James I.
publicSummary: The failed Gunpowder Plot turned religious fear into harder state suspicion and sharper anti-Catholic politics.
consequences: stronger public rituals of Protestant loyalty
deeper anxiety about conspiracy and internal enemies
madePossible: more durable association between Protestant identity and state loyalty
destroyedOrDisplaced: hopes that James I would settle confessional tension quietly
sourcesOrRationale: Based on Britannica's Gunpowder Plot entry and UK Parliament's overview of the plot and its aftermath.
